romania.title: Luna iulie aduce o nouă ediție FIDELIS. Românii pot investi în titluri de stat cu dobânzi de până la 7,55%
romania.summary: Ministerul Finanțelor lansează, în perioada 3-10 iulie, o nouă ediție a Programului FIDELIS, prin care populația poate investi în titluri de stat denominate în lei și euro, cu dobânzi neimpozabile de până la 7,55% pentru emisiunile în moneda națională și de până la 6,20% pentru cele în euro.

Potrivit lui Grindeanu, avionul Nordis e de două ore la Otopeni şi chiar nu mai poate sta mult să îl aştepte. „Mă sună omul ăla că e pe avarii pe pistă de două ore […] The post Grindeanu cere să i se spună clar dacă va fi premier sau nu, că îl aşteaptă avionul Nordis pe avarii appeared first on Times New Roman .

medical.title: The brain's language network is more extensive than previously thought
medical.summary: For decades, neuroscientists have known that specific regions in the brain's left hemisphere are responsible for processing language. However, a new study by MIT researchers shows that language processing also occurs in many other parts of the brain.

medical.title: Stronger focus on nutrition needed in eating disorder recovery
medical.summary: Overreliance on body mass index (BMI) to measure recovery from eating disorders has sparked calls for better assessment methods. University of Queensland researchers reviewed 60 studies from five databases across 16 countries between 1990 and 2025 to understand more about how nutrition fits into eating disorder treatment. Their findings are published in the journal Eating Disorders.

medical.title: Researchers to teens: Get to bed—it's good for your blood sugar
medical.summary: If you're 18, getting a bit more sleep could matter more than you think. New research from the University of Copenhagen and COPSAC shows that even in healthy young people, longer nights of sleep are linked to more stable blood sugar levels the next day. What's more, the relationship appears to go both ways. The findings suggest that sleep may play an important role in our metabolism long before conditions such as diabetes develop.

medical.title: Should pregnant women worry about taking Tylenol? 20-year sibling-matched study finds no link to autism or ADHD
medical.summary: Tylenol (also known as acetaminophen or paracetamol) is one of the most widely used over-the-counter options for easing pain and reducing fever, including during pregnancy. More recently, safety concerns around use during pregnancy have gained attention, with claims of a possible link between prenatal exposure and autism in children increasingly appearing in news headlines and prime-time debates.

medical.title: Your dominant hand is made, not born, experiments suggest
medical.summary: Most people favor one hand, and that hand tends to be the better one for writing, throwing and managing chopsticks. The long-standing view is that the dominant hand is "born" more capable, its skills rooted in a brain hemisphere specialized for motor control. A new study in PNAS argues that this difference in skill is not innate at all. It is the consequence of a lifetime of practice, and it surfaces only when we pick up a tool.

medical.title: Researchers improve analysis of molecules linked to Alzheimer's disease
medical.summary: Researchers at the FAMU-FSU College of Engineering and the National High Magnetic Field Laboratory have shown how higher magnetic fields can improve analysis of the molecules linked to Alzheimer's disease, a finding that could aid the development of future treatments.

medical.title: Running on a treadmill is easier than running outside. Does that matter?
medical.summary: You hop on the treadmill, set your pace and easily knock out a couple of kilometers (about 1.2 miles). Then, a few days later, you head outside to run the same distance. You try to keep the same speed you did at the gym, but it destroys you.

medical.title: How athletes envision the future of sport during climate change
medical.summary: There is an urgent need for greater climate action across the sports industry, emphasizing the critical role athletes can play in shaping a more sustainable future for sport, according to a new briefing report.

medical.title: Urgent call for better access to personalized cancer care
medical.summary: A University of Queensland study has found that patient access to one of the most exciting frontiers in cancer treatment is being stalled because health systems struggle to accurately assess its cost-effectiveness. The study, "Assessing Value for Money in Theranostic Nuclear Medicine: A Systematic Review," was published in the Journal of Nuclear Medicine.

medical.title: Substantial rise in antinausea medicine use during pregnancy, New Zealand study shows
medical.summary: There has been a fivefold increase in the use of antinausea medicines during Aotearoa New Zealand pregnancies, a University of Otago—Ōtākou Whakaihu Waka-led study has found.

medical.title: DNA methylation patterns may explain why some prostate cancers turn lethal
medical.summary: The prostate is the single organ most frequently afflicted by cancer in men. Prostate cancer affects approximately 4 million American men, with another 330,000 men expected to be diagnosed with the condition this year alone.

medical.title: Yale study finds nearly half of older adults improved with age
medical.summary: A long-term Yale study is challenging one of the biggest myths about aging. Nearly half of adults over 65 improved physically, mentally, or both over time, despite the common belief that aging means constant decline. Researchers found that people with more positive attitudes about getting older were significantly more likely to show these gains.

medical.title: Scientists discover neurons must break their DNA to build the brain
medical.summary: As newborn neurons make their way through the developing brain, they must squeeze through incredibly tight spaces to reach their final destinations. Researchers discovered that this physical journey routinely causes some of the most severe forms of DNA damage—double-strand breaks—yet the young brain has evolved an impressive ability to repair the damage almost immediately.

medical.title: Major review finds vaping likely causes lung and oral cancer
medical.summary: Researchers have concluded that nicotine vapes are likely to cause lung and oral cancers, based on evidence ranging from human biomarkers to animal and laboratory studies. The findings challenge the idea that vaping is a harmless alternative to smoking and suggest health risks may be emerging much sooner than many expected.

science.title: 400-year-old painting reveals a bat's secret diet
science.summary: Natural historians have many observational techniques in their toolkit for learning about the natural world: tagging animals with tracking devices, recording sounds, analyzing droppings or simply watching and counting. As technology has advanced, these methods have grown far more precise and wide-reaching, letting researchers capture details that were once impossible to detect. Even today, however, these cutting-edge tools aren't the only route to discovery.

science.title: Are you 'happee' or are you 'happeh?' Study links accents to social classes
science.summary: Our accents give away more about social class than we think, according to new research led by Lancaster University. The new sociolinguistic study focusing on Mancunian accents, published in the journal, Language Variation and Change, shows that the final vowel in words like happy, baby, chilly and city—known to linguists as "the happy vowel"—varies clearly by social class across Manchester.

science.title: What made trees possible? New research points to drought
science.summary: A study is reframing a fundamental question in plant evolution: What made trees possible? Researchers from Cal Poly Humboldt, Yale University, the University of Hohenheim in Germany and the Czech Academy of Sciences set out to understand how trees evolved and what allowed them to become some of the largest and longest-living organisms on Earth.

science.title: Were Clovis foragers in Late Pleistocene North America big-game hunters, or just big-game scavengers?
science.summary: There are currently 15 well-documented Late Pleistocene localities in North America in which Clovis points are found associated with proboscidean remains (of mammoth, mastodon and gomphothere). Archaeologists routinely assume these localities represent evidence that Clovis people hunted these multi-ton animals, and in turn invoke that evidence to claim humans had a role in the extinction of these large mammals.

science.title: Why taking a sick day depends on more than being sick
science.summary: As winter illness spreads and households face cost-of-living pressure, many Australians cannot treat a sick day as a simple health decision. They may be too sick to work—but their job is too insecure to stay home. New research led by UTS shows the decision to take sick leave is heavily influenced by pay, job security and gender.

science.title: Webb reveals merger scars in galaxies that stopped forming stars 9 billion years ago
science.summary: Research has shed new light on why some distant galaxies suddenly stop forming stars. An international team led by astronomers at the University of Nottingham has used the James Webb Space Telescope to study a large sample of recently "quenched" galaxies in the distant universe, observed around nine billion years ago. Their findings appear in the Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society.

science.title: NASA’s TESS Mission Finds Planetary System in New Way
science.summary: For the first time, NASA’s TESS (Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) mission has identified a planet orbiting a distant star thanks to ripples in space-time. Unlike the star-hugging transiting planets TESS regularly reveals, the newfound world is a super-Jupiter orbiting far from its host star. “When TESS launched, no one expected it to ever be capable […]

science.title: Galaxy groups hiding in the universe's emptiest places
science.summary: Imagine standing in the emptiest place the universe has to offer, a stretch of cosmic ocean so vast that light takes tens of millions of years to cross it, and yet still finding company. That is the puzzle behind a new study built on the Calar Alto Void Integral field Treasury surveY, or CAVITY, posted to the arXiv preprint server.

science.title: Think human anatomy is finished? Scientists say think again
science.summary: Despite centuries of study, scientists are still finding new details and even overlooked structures within the human body. As researchers explore anatomical differences between individuals, it’s becoming clear that the body is far more complex—and less fully understood—than textbooks suggest.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Luna iulie aduce o nouă ediție FIDELIS. Românii pot investi în titluri de stat cu dobânzi de până la 7,55%

mix_items.summary: Un show despre iubire, anxietăți și decizii proaste luate cu încredere.Dacă ai avut vreodată: un date dubios,anxietate înainte să răspunzi la „bună, ce faci?”, sau ai spus „nu mă mai atașez” și după primul date visai deja la nuntă… show-ul ăsta e pentru tine!Locurile sunt limitate, atmosfera va fi intimă, iar trandafirii sunt doar decorativi. […] The post Turneu Stand-up Comedy cu Călin Gheorghiu: Caut iubită! appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Grindeanu cere să i se spună clar dacă va fi premier sau nu, că îl aşteaptă avionul Nordis pe avarii
mix_items.summary: În prag de vacanţă parlamentară, şeful PSD şi-a pierdut răbdarea şi cere să-i spunem clar dacă îl punem premier sau nu! Potrivit lui Grindeanu, avionul Nordis e de două ore la Otopeni şi chiar nu mai poate sta mult să îl aştepte. „Mă sună omul ăla că e pe avarii pe pistă de două ore […] The post Grindeanu cere să i se spună clar dacă va fi premier sau nu, că îl aşteaptă avionul Nordis pe avarii appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Dacă mai era vreo îndoială că preşedintele face jocurile PSD-ului, acesta tocmai a spulberat-o! Nicuşor Dan a fost văzut azi la un grup şcolar din Buzău, unde surse politice spun că ar fi dat examenul de Bacalaureat la matematică în locul lui Marcel Ciolacu. „Da, poate l-am ajutat pe Ciolacu dându-mă drept el la Bac, […] The post Omul PSD-ului până la capăt: Nicuşor Dan a dat azi Bacul la mate în locul lui Ciolacu appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Liceul Politehnica București, Opera Comică pentru Copii și Programul Național Cantus Mundi lansează primul model de educație liceală dedicat industriei spectacolelor. Pentru prima dată în România, elevii vor putea urma,… Articolul Primul liceu din România unde se vor pregăti artiști de musical și specialiști în sunet și lumini pentru spectacole apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Ediția a VIII-a a Festivalului SoNoRo Musikland va avea loc în perioada 17-27 iulie 2026 și se va desfășura în Sibiu, Avrig, Porumbacu de Sus, Copșa Mare, Sighișoara, Meșendorf, Criț, Viscri și Poiana Brașov.… Articolul Ediția a VIII-a a Festivalului SoNoRo Musikland va avea loc în perioada 17-27 iulie 2026 și se va desfășura în Sibiu, Avrig, Porumbacu de Sus, Copșa Mare, Sighișoara, Meșendorf, Criț, Viscri și Poiana Brașov. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: După o creștere de 38% a volumelor de roșii românești achiziționate în 2025 față de anul anterior, Lidl estimează pentru sezonul 2026 o nouă creștere, de aproximativ 30%, ceea ce… Articolul 100% roșii românești la raft: Lidl România marchează un an record al achizițiilor de la producătorii locali apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Centrul de Proiecte al Municipiului Timișoara organizează o serie de două ateliere dedicate medierii culturale, adresate profesioniștilor din sectorul cultural, studenților și tuturor celor interesați de modul în care arta… Articolul Ateliere de mediere culturală | Practici de conectare între artă, spații și comunități, de la Bienala de la Veneția la Spațiile pentru Comunitate ale Timișoarei apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.title: The brain's language network is more extensive than previously thought
mix_items.summary: For decades, neuroscientists have known that specific regions in the brain's left hemisphere are responsible for processing language. However, a new study by MIT researchers shows that language processing also occurs in many other parts of the brain.

mix_items.title: Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026
mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.summary: București, 20 mai 2026: Fundația World Vision România, împreună cu Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași, a inaugurat o seră... Articolul World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. O seră didactică modernizată cu tehnologii de Agricultură 4.0, inaugurată la Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Stronger focus on nutrition needed in eating disorder recovery
mix_items.summary: Overreliance on body mass index (BMI) to measure recovery from eating disorders has sparked calls for better assessment methods. University of Queensland researchers reviewed 60 studies from five databases across 16 countries between 1990 and 2025 to understand more about how nutrition fits into eating disorder treatment. Their findings are published in the journal Eating Disorders.

mix_items.title: Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor
mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.summary: În România, mulți copii încă se tem să vorbească despre abuzurile prin care trec. Conform unui studiu al Fundației World... Articolul Doar 1 din 2 copii ar anunța poliția în cazul unui abuz. World Vision România: Copiii care raportează trebuie sprijiniți și încurajați, nu umiliți public. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: 400-year-old painting reveals a bat's secret diet
mix_items.summary: Natural historians have many observational techniques in their toolkit for learning about the natural world: tagging animals with tracking devices, recording sounds, analyzing droppings or simply watching and counting. As technology has advanced, these methods have grown far more precise and wide-reaching, letting researchers capture details that were once impossible to detect. Even today, however, these cutting-edge tools aren't the only route to discovery.

mix_items.title: Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor
mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.summary: Locație: București (Satul SOS) Nivel carieră: Mid-level (3-5 ani experiență) Departament: Financiar / Contabilitate Tip job: Full-time Cine suntem noi? La SOS Satele Copiilor România, oferim o familie copiilor ajunși... The post Contabil appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Are you 'happee' or are you 'happeh?' Study links accents to social classes
mix_items.summary: Our accents give away more about social class than we think, according to new research led by Lancaster University. The new sociolinguistic study focusing on Mancunian accents, published in the journal, Language Variation and Change, shows that the final vowel in words like happy, baby, chilly and city—known to linguists as "the happy vowel"—varies clearly by social class across Manchester.

mix_items.title: World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România
mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... Articolul World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi
mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Researchers to teens: Get to bed—it's good for your blood sugar
mix_items.summary: If you're 18, getting a bit more sleep could matter more than you think. New research from the University of Copenhagen and COPSAC shows that even in healthy young people, longer nights of sleep are linked to more stable blood sugar levels the next day. What's more, the relationship appears to go both ways. The findings suggest that sleep may play an important role in our metabolism long before conditions such as diabetes develop.

mix_items.title: Should pregnant women worry about taking Tylenol? 20-year sibling-matched study finds no link to autism or ADHD
mix_items.summary: Tylenol (also known as acetaminophen or paracetamol) is one of the most widely used over-the-counter options for easing pain and reducing fever, including during pregnancy. More recently, safety concerns around use during pregnancy have gained attention, with claims of a possible link between prenatal exposure and autism in children increasingly appearing in news headlines and prime-time debates.

mix_items.title: Your dominant hand is made, not born, experiments suggest
mix_items.summary: Most people favor one hand, and that hand tends to be the better one for writing, throwing and managing chopsticks. The long-standing view is that the dominant hand is "born" more capable, its skills rooted in a brain hemisphere specialized for motor control. A new study in PNAS argues that this difference in skill is not innate at all. It is the consequence of a lifetime of practice, and it surfaces only when we pick up a tool.

mix_items.title: Researchers improve analysis of molecules linked to Alzheimer's disease
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the FAMU-FSU College of Engineering and the National High Magnetic Field Laboratory have shown how higher magnetic fields can improve analysis of the molecules linked to Alzheimer's disease, a finding that could aid the development of future treatments.

mix_items.title: What made trees possible? New research points to drought
mix_items.summary: A study is reframing a fundamental question in plant evolution: What made trees possible? Researchers from Cal Poly Humboldt, Yale University, the University of Hohenheim in Germany and the Czech Academy of Sciences set out to understand how trees evolved and what allowed them to become some of the largest and longest-living organisms on Earth.

mix_items.title: Running on a treadmill is easier than running outside. Does that matter?
mix_items.summary: You hop on the treadmill, set your pace and easily knock out a couple of kilometers (about 1.2 miles). Then, a few days later, you head outside to run the same distance. You try to keep the same speed you did at the gym, but it destroys you.

mix_items.title: Were Clovis foragers in Late Pleistocene North America big-game hunters, or just big-game scavengers?
mix_items.summary: There are currently 15 well-documented Late Pleistocene localities in North America in which Clovis points are found associated with proboscidean remains (of mammoth, mastodon and gomphothere). Archaeologists routinely assume these localities represent evidence that Clovis people hunted these multi-ton animals, and in turn invoke that evidence to claim humans had a role in the extinction of these large mammals.

mix_items.title: Why taking a sick day depends on more than being sick
mix_items.summary: As winter illness spreads and households face cost-of-living pressure, many Australians cannot treat a sick day as a simple health decision. They may be too sick to work—but their job is too insecure to stay home. New research led by UTS shows the decision to take sick leave is heavily influenced by pay, job security and gender.

mix_items.title: Webb reveals merger scars in galaxies that stopped forming stars 9 billion years ago
mix_items.summary: Research has shed new light on why some distant galaxies suddenly stop forming stars. An international team led by astronomers at the University of Nottingham has used the James Webb Space Telescope to study a large sample of recently "quenched" galaxies in the distant universe, observed around nine billion years ago. Their findings appear in the Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society.

mix_items.title: NASA’s TESS Mission Finds Planetary System in New Way
mix_items.summary: For the first time, NASA’s TESS (Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) mission has identified a planet orbiting a distant star thanks to ripples in space-time. Unlike the star-hugging transiting planets TESS regularly reveals, the newfound world is a super-Jupiter orbiting far from its host star. “When TESS launched, no one expected it to ever be capable […]

mix_items.title: How athletes envision the future of sport during climate change
mix_items.summary: There is an urgent need for greater climate action across the sports industry, emphasizing the critical role athletes can play in shaping a more sustainable future for sport, according to a new briefing report.

mix_items.title: Urgent call for better access to personalized cancer care
mix_items.summary: A University of Queensland study has found that patient access to one of the most exciting frontiers in cancer treatment is being stalled because health systems struggle to accurately assess its cost-effectiveness. The study, "Assessing Value for Money in Theranostic Nuclear Medicine: A Systematic Review," was published in the Journal of Nuclear Medicine.

mix_items.title: Substantial rise in antinausea medicine use during pregnancy, New Zealand study shows
mix_items.summary: There has been a fivefold increase in the use of antinausea medicines during Aotearoa New Zealand pregnancies, a University of Otago—Ōtākou Whakaihu Waka-led study has found.

mix_items.title: DNA methylation patterns may explain why some prostate cancers turn lethal
mix_items.summary: The prostate is the single organ most frequently afflicted by cancer in men. Prostate cancer affects approximately 4 million American men, with another 330,000 men expected to be diagnosed with the condition this year alone.

mix_items.title: Galaxy groups hiding in the universe's emptiest places
mix_items.summary: Imagine standing in the emptiest place the universe has to offer, a stretch of cosmic ocean so vast that light takes tens of millions of years to cross it, and yet still finding company. That is the puzzle behind a new study built on the Calar Alto Void Integral field Treasury surveY, or CAVITY, posted to the arXiv preprint server.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: Yale study finds nearly half of older adults improved with age
mix_items.summary: A long-term Yale study is challenging one of the biggest myths about aging. Nearly half of adults over 65 improved physically, mentally, or both over time, despite the common belief that aging means constant decline. Researchers found that people with more positive attitudes about getting older were significantly more likely to show these gains.

mix_items.title: Think human anatomy is finished? Scientists say think again
mix_items.summary: Despite centuries of study, scientists are still finding new details and even overlooked structures within the human body. As researchers explore anatomical differences between individuals, it’s becoming clear that the body is far more complex—and less fully understood—than textbooks suggest.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover neurons must break their DNA to build the brain
mix_items.summary: As newborn neurons make their way through the developing brain, they must squeeze through incredibly tight spaces to reach their final destinations. Researchers discovered that this physical journey routinely causes some of the most severe forms of DNA damage—double-strand breaks—yet the young brain has evolved an impressive ability to repair the damage almost immediately.

mix_items.title: Major review finds vaping likely causes lung and oral cancer
mix_items.summary: Researchers have concluded that nicotine vapes are likely to cause lung and oral cancers, based on evidence ranging from human biomarkers to animal and laboratory studies. The findings challenge the idea that vaping is a harmless alternative to smoking and suggest health risks may be emerging much sooner than many expected.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Cum merge dieta? - Excelent. O admir de la distanta.

satire_ro.title: Omul PSD-ului până la capăt: Nicuşor Dan a dat azi Bacul la mate în locul lui Ciolacu
